# RacePlex

> Open-source, offline-first lap timing and telemetry analysis for electric skateboards. Import GPS logs, get lap and sector times, speed-coloured track maps and lap comparisons — all in the browser, with no upload and no account.

RacePlex is a Progressive Web App for electric skateboard riders. Telemetry parsing, lap detection and visualisation all happen locally in the browser (IndexedDB). No server processes your data. There is no paid tier and no feature gating.

Supported formats: **VESC Tool CSV**, **RaceBox CSV**, **GPX**, **GoPro `.mp4`** (GPS is read from the video's GPMF metadata track, in the browser; chapter-split recordings fold into one session), **FIT** (Garmin, Wahoo, Coros, Suunto — these log at about 1 Hz), **RaceChrono CSV v3**, **VBO**, **NMEA 0183**, **UBX**, and, through a generic importer with a user-correctable column mapper, most other delimited logs containing a latitude and a longitude (Float Control, pOnewheel, Metr, TrackAddict, Qstarz). The car and kart formats inherited from upstream also work: MoTeC i2, AiM MyChron (CSV and XRK/XRZ), Alfano, iRacing, and Dove/Dovex.

Live capture: RacePlex records directly from a **RaceBox** or a **Dragy** over Web Bluetooth (Chrome or Edge, desktop or Android), with no file to export first. A phone's own GPS can also record.

Features specific to electric skateboards:

- **ESC telemetry charted with GPS.** A VESC log imports with motor current, battery current, battery voltage, duty cycle, ERPM and temperatures on the same timeline as the GPS trace. A nosedive appears as a duty-cycle spike.
- **Point-to-point courses.** RacePlex supports separate start and finish lines, for a hill run, slalom or drag pass that finishes somewhere other than where it started.
- **Lap timing with no setup.** When a datalog records where its timing lines were, RacePlex reads them back and times the run: from GPX Start/Finish waypoints, or by reconstructing the lines from RaceBox's lap-number column.
- **A stance tool.** Foot position, weight distribution, and the hardest stop a board can actually make — which depends on which wheels the motors drive, because an eskate's motors are its only brakes. A rear-driven board (nearly all of them) unloads the very wheels doing the braking, so it tops out around 0.17 g and the rear slides; it cannot endo under motor braking on any geometry. An all-wheel board keeps the full ~0.6 g of urethane grip available and does pitch, with the rear lifting at around 0.37 g.

Other features: automatic track and course detection, sector splits and optimal lap, reference-lap overlays and pace delta, speed heatmaps on a Leaflet map, braking-zone analysis, G-force visualisation, video export with overlays via WebCodecs, and Web Bluetooth session download from supported loggers.

Notes: Dragy has no CSV export — record from it live over Bluetooth, or use the dragy·Lap app's `.vbo` export. The GoPro HERO12 has no GPS. FIT devices (Garmin, Wahoo, Coros) log at about 1 Hz, which is roughly 11 m between fixes at 40 km/h — enough to plot a ride, too coarse to place a braking point.

## Pages

- [Home](https://beadon.github.io/RacePlex/): The app. Import a datalog, then explore the race-line map, charts, lap times and lap comparisons. Installs as a PWA on a phone (Add to Home Screen) and runs offline afterwards.

## Optional

- [GitHub — RacePlex](https://github.com/beadon/RacePlex): Source code (GPL-3.0).
- [Telemetry format reference](https://github.com/beadon/RacePlex/blob/main/docs/research/FORMATS.md): Documentation for every format RacePlex reads, including the details that are easy to get wrong (VBO stores coordinates in minutes, positive-west; RaceBox's CSV speed column carries no unit and is not in m/s).
- [GitHub — Dove's DataViewer](https://github.com/TheAngryRaven/DovesDataViewer): The upstream project RacePlex is forked from.
